note_data.title: Atomic structure
keyPoints: An **atom** consists of a nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by electrons in shells
The **nucleus** is positively charged and contains most of the mass of the atom
**Protons** have a relative charge of +1 and relative mass of 1
**Neutrons** have no charge (neutral) and relative mass of 1
**Electrons** have a relative charge of -1 and negligible mass
**Atomic number** equals the number of protons in the nucleus
**Mass number** equals the total number of protons and neutrons
**Isotopes** are atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
**Electron configuration** shows how electrons are arranged in shells around the nucleus
The first shell holds a maximum of 2 electrons, second and third shells hold a maximum of 8 electrons each

definitions.definition: The smallest particle of an element that can take part in a chemical reaction
A substance that contains only one type of atom
Atoms of the same element with the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom
The total number of protons and neutrons in the nucleus of an atom

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give the main features or characteristics in logical order
State similarities and differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Chlorine has two isotopes: chlorine-35 and chlorine-37. Both isotopes have 17 protons. State the number of neutrons in each isotope and explain why they are isotopes of the same element.
modelAnswer.answer: Chlorine-35 has 18 neutrons. Chlorine-37 has 20 neutrons. They are isotopes of the same element because they have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

commonMistakes: Never say electrons are 'in the nucleus' — electrons are in shells around the nucleus
Students often confuse mass number with atomic number — mass number includes neutrons
Never say isotopes have 'different atomic numbers' — isotopes have the same atomic number
Students forget that atoms are neutral overall — equal numbers of protons and electrons
Never say 'molecules' when describing atomic structure — use 'atoms'
examinerTips: This topic appears in almost every paper — master the definitions
Always show working when calculating numbers of subatomic particles
Draw clear, labelled diagrams of atomic structure for full marks
Remember to use the notation ᴬₓE where A is mass number and Z is atomic number
formulas: Number of neutrons = Mass number - Atomic number
Number of electrons = Number of protons (in neutral atoms)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Diagram showing nucleus at center with protons (+) and neutrons (0) inside, and electrons (-) arranged in shells around the nucleus. Label nucleus, electrons, and shell numbers.
pastPaperRefs: Electronic configuration was tested in M/J 2022 P2 Q2 (3 marks) — they asked students to determine the electronic configuration of ion X²⁺ that has the same configuration as Kr
Unpaired electrons appeared in O/N 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — candidates had to identify which atom has exactly three unpaired electrons in ground state
Relative atomic mass calculation was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — students calculated Ar of metal M from given data and identified the element
Full electronic configuration was examined in O/N 2022 P2 Q1b (2 marks) — required writing complete electron configuration for ²⁵Mg₁₂ using s,p,d notation
Element identification through quantitative analysis appeared in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — students identified unknown element E in molecule containing N, 3O, 3H atoms

workedExample.question: Ion X²⁺ has the same electronic configuration as Kr (krypton). State the electronic configuration of the neutral atom X. (3 marks)
steps: Step 1: Find the electronic configuration of Kr. Kr has atomic number 36, so its configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d¹⁰ 4p⁶
Step 2: Since X²⁺ has the same configuration as Kr, the X²⁺ ion has 36 electrons
Step 3: The neutral atom X must have 2 more electrons than X²⁺ (since it lost 2 electrons to form X²⁺)
Step 4: Therefore, neutral atom X has 38 electrons, so its configuration is 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s² 3p⁶ 4s² 3d¹⁰ 4p⁶ 5s²
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for Kr configuration (36 electrons), 1 mark for recognizing X²⁺ = 36 electrons so X = 38 electrons, 1 mark for correct final configuration

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the order of filling 4s and 3d orbitals' — always remember 4s fills before 3d, but 4s empties before 3d when forming ions

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently gave abbreviated electronic configurations when full configurations were requested' — always write out complete 1s² 2s² 2p⁶... format unless specifically asked for noble gas notation

Examiner Report 2024: 'Common error was not accounting for charge when determining electron number in ions' — always add electrons for negative ions and subtract for positive ions before writing configuration
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw atomic orbital diagrams showing electron spin arrangements using boxes and arrows. Draw energy levels as horizontal lines with 1s lowest, then 2s, 2p (three boxes at same level), 3s, 3p (three boxes), 4s, 3d (five boxes), 4p etc. Show electrons as arrows: ↑ for first electron in orbital, ↑↓ for paired electrons. Include labels for each sublevel and demonstrate Hund's rule by filling orbitals singly before pairing.
note_data.quickRecap: Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p. Each orbital holds maximum 2 electrons with opposite spins. For ions, remove electrons from highest energy orbitals first (4s before 3d). Electronic configuration determines chemical properties and bonding behavior.
